title: Doctor Zhivago
description: A sweeping novel of love, revolution, conscience, and the moral wreckage of Soviet history.

cover.alt: Doctor Zhivago cover thumbnail from Wikipedia

overview.englishDescription: A sweeping novel of love, revolution, conscience, and the moral wreckage of Soviet history.
banningParagraphs: Doctor Zhivago entered censorship debates as a novel associated with revolution, individual conscience, and anti totalitarianism. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around political dissent and anti state.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1957-1988 in Soviet Union, where Soviet literary authorities banned publication. The novel's moral skepticism about revolutionary history made it unacceptable at home. The Nobel scandal around Pasternak amplified the ban into an international free-expression story.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism
counterReadings.author: Hannah Arendt
counterReadings.note: A foundational analysis of state terror, propaganda, and ideological conformity.

counterReadings.title: On Tyranny
counterReadings.author: Timothy Snyder
counterReadings.note: A short modern guide to resisting authoritarian politics and controlled public discourse.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
